slides.title: After the Mycenaean World
slides.text: By 1000 BCE, the great Mycenaean palace system of the Late Bronze Age had already fallen apart, and the Greek-speaking world was living through a long period of recovery. Political authority was local, literacy had largely disappeared with the loss of Linear B administration, and many communities were smaller and less centralized than those of the earlier palace age. Yet this was not simply a collapse into emptiness: village life continued, regional traditions survived, and over time the foundations of a new Greek world slowly took shape.

slides.title: The Rise of the Polis
slides.text: From roughly the 8th century BCE onward, Greece experienced demographic growth, renewed long-distance trade, and the emergence of the polis, or city-state, as its defining political form. Athens, Sparta, Corinth, Thebes, and many smaller states developed their own constitutions, elites, and civic identities. At the same time, shared language, religious festivals, heroic traditions, and sanctuaries such as Delphi and Olympia gave the Greek world a broad cultural unity without ever creating a single Greek state.

slides.title: Colonization and a Wider Greek World
slides.text: During the Archaic period, Greeks founded settlements across the Mediterranean and Black Sea, from southern Italy and Sicily to the coasts of Asia Minor and beyond. These colonies spread Greek language, religion, and trade networks far beyond the Aegean, while also bringing wealth and outside influences back into mainland Greece. This wider world helped make Greek civilization dynamic, competitive, and deeply connected to neighboring peoples.

slides.title: Law, Society, and Competing Systems
slides.text: Archaic Greece was politically experimental. Some cities were ruled by aristocracies, some by tyrants, and some gradually opened space for broader citizen participation. Sparta built a famously militarized society centered on a small citizen elite and the labor of the helots, while Athens moved in the direction of wider political participation through reforms associated with figures such as Solon and Cleisthenes. These competing systems would shape Greek politics for centuries.

slides.title: The Persian Wars
slides.text: In the early 5th century BCE, the expanding Achaemenid Empire sought to bring mainland Greece more firmly into its sphere, and several Greek states resisted. The battles of Marathon, Thermopylae, Salamis, and Plataea became defining episodes in Greek memory because they were later presented as victories of free Greek communities over imperial domination. The wars did not end Greek disunity, but they gave many Greeks a stronger sense of participating in a shared civilizational story.

slides.title: Athens and the Classical Age
slides.text: After the Persian Wars, Athens rose to extraordinary prominence through the Delian League, naval power, and the wealth of empire. The city became a center of tragedy, philosophy, architecture, rhetoric, and democratic participation, producing figures such as Pericles, Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides, Socrates, and many others. Later generations would look back on 5th-century Athens as one of the most influential political and cultural moments in world history.

slides.title: Sparta and Greek Rivalry
slides.text: Athens never stood alone as the model of Greek civilization. Sparta represented a sharply different political and social order, one built around military discipline, oligarchic control, and dominance over neighboring populations. The rivalry between Athens and Sparta shaped Greek diplomacy for decades and framed the Greek world as a competitive system of proud and often suspicious states.

slides.title: The Peloponnesian War
slides.text: From 431 to 404 BCE, the Peloponnesian War devastated much of Greece. Athens and Sparta led opposing alliances, and the long conflict drained manpower, wealth, and political stability across the Greek world. The war damaged Athens deeply, weakened confidence in the polis system, and left a legacy of exhaustion that later powers would exploit.

slides.title: The Fourth Century Crisis
slides.text: After the Peloponnesian War, no Greek state could establish stable hegemony for long. Sparta briefly dominated, then Thebes rose under leaders such as Epaminondas, and Athens partly recovered, but the system remained fractured and conflict-ridden. Constant warfare among the major powers left the southern Greek states vulnerable to the growing strength of Macedon in the north.

slides.title: Philip II and Macedonian Supremacy
slides.text: Philip II transformed Macedon into the strongest military state in the Greek world through reforms in infantry tactics, cavalry organization, siegecraft, and diplomacy. His victory at Chaeronea in 338 BCE broke the independence of the leading southern city-states and established Macedonian supremacy over Greece. Greek political life continued, but the era of fully autonomous great city-states had effectively ended.

slides.title: Alexander and the Hellenistic Explosion
slides.text: Alexander the Great carried Macedonian and Greek power across the Persian Empire and as far as Egypt, Central Asia, and northwestern India. His campaigns did not create a unified Greek nation, but they did spread Greek language, political ideas, and elite culture across a vast imperial space. After his death in 323 BCE, the Hellenistic kingdoms preserved this mixed Greek-eastern world for centuries.

slides.title: The Hellenistic Greek World
slides.text: In the Hellenistic age, Greek-speaking dynasties ruled major successor kingdoms in Egypt, Asia, and the Near East, while old mainland centers adapted to a changed political world. Greek cities remained important cultural centers, and philosophy, science, art, and scholarship flourished in places such as Alexandria and Pergamon. Greece itself was no longer the unquestioned political center of Greek civilization, but Greek culture reached farther than ever before.

slides.title: Roman Conquest and Continuity
slides.text: Rome gradually intervened in Greek affairs and eventually subdued the major Hellenistic powers. By the 2nd century BCE, Greece had become part of Rome's imperial order, yet conquest did not erase Greek prestige. Instead, Greek language, literature, philosophy, and education became central to elite Roman culture, and Greek cities continued to play important intellectual and religious roles within the empire.

slides.title: Christianity and the Eastern Empire
slides.text: As Christianity spread and the Roman world changed, the Greek-speaking eastern provinces became increasingly important. When the empire's center shifted toward Constantinople, the Greek lands found themselves ever more closely linked to the eastern imperial sphere. Over time, Greek language and Orthodox Christianity became defining pillars of the civilization later called Byzantine.

slides.title: Greece in the Byzantine Age
slides.text: For many centuries, the Greek lands formed a major part of the Byzantine Empire, though not always its secure heartland. They experienced invasions, administrative reforms, periods of prosperity, religious controversy, and close integration with the wider eastern Mediterranean economy. Byzantine rule also preserved classical learning, developed medieval Greek culture, and tied Greek identity strongly to Orthodox Christianity.

slides.title: The Fourth Crusade and Fragmentation
slides.text: The sack of Constantinople in 1204 by the Fourth Crusade was a major rupture. Byzantine authority shattered, and Greek territories were divided among Latin rulers, Venetian interests, and Greek successor states. Although Byzantine rule was restored in Constantinople in 1261, the empire never fully recovered its former strength, and the Greek world remained politically fractured.

slides.title: Ottoman Rule
slides.text: From the late 14th and 15th centuries onward, most Greek lands gradually came under Ottoman rule. The conquest did not erase Greek language or religion, since Orthodox institutions, monastic centers, and communal structures preserved important forms of continuity. At the same time, Greek merchants, scholars, sailors, and local elites adapted to imperial realities and remained active across the eastern Mediterranean.

slides.title: Memory, Faith, and Identity
slides.text: During the Ottoman centuries, Greek identity was shaped by several overlapping inheritances: ancient Hellenic memory, Byzantine Orthodox tradition, local regional loyalties, and newer ideas circulating in Europe. The church, diaspora communities, and commercial networks all helped preserve language and education. By the 18th century, the Greek Enlightenment was connecting older traditions to modern political ideas about nationhood and liberation.

slides.title: The Greek War of Independence
slides.text: In 1821, revolution broke out against Ottoman rule in several Greek regions. The struggle was violent, fragmented, and often internally divided, but it also attracted strong philhellenic support from Europe, where ancient Greece held enormous symbolic prestige. By the late 1820s and early 1830s, outside intervention and military developments helped secure an independent Greek state.

slides.title: Building a Modern State
slides.text: The new Greek kingdom was much smaller than the full Greek-speaking world imagined by many nationalists, and state-building proved difficult. Greece had to create central institutions, establish administration, define citizenship, and manage tensions between local power networks and imported monarchical rule. Yet the kingdom also became the political core of modern Greek national life.

slides.title: Expansion in the Nineteenth Century
slides.text: During the 19th century, Greece gradually expanded its borders and incorporated additional Greek populations through diplomacy, revolt, and war. National politics were strongly shaped by the Megali Idea, the ambition to unite all historically Greek or Greek-speaking lands in one state. This ideal linked modern nationalism to memories of both ancient Greece and Byzantium.

slides.title: The Balkan Wars and New Territory
slides.text: The Balkan Wars of 1912-1913 dramatically enlarged Greece and brought major gains in Macedonia, Epirus, and the Aegean. These victories strengthened the kingdom and transformed its demographic and strategic position. At the same time, they intensified regional rivalries and set the stage for new conflicts over the future of the eastern Mediterranean.

slides.title: War, Schism, and Catastrophe
slides.text: World War I and its aftermath deepened internal political division in Greece through the National Schism between supporters of King Constantine and Eleftherios Venizelos. Greek ambitions in Asia Minor briefly seemed within reach after the war, but the Greco-Turkish War ended in military defeat in 1922. The resulting population exchange between Greece and Turkey transformed the country socially, economically, and emotionally.

slides.title: Occupation, Resistance, and Civil War
slides.text: In World War II, Greece first resisted the Italian invasion and then endured German occupation, famine, brutality, and destruction. Resistance movements grew powerful, but wartime divisions fed directly into the Greek Civil War after liberation. The civil war was one of the earliest and sharpest Cold War conflicts, leaving deep political wounds that shaped Greek life for decades.

slides.title: Postwar Recovery and the Junta
slides.text: After the civil war, Greece aligned with the Western bloc and pursued reconstruction, migration, and economic development. But political instability persisted, and in 1967 a military junta seized power. The dictatorship repressed dissent and damaged the country's political life, while the Cyprus crisis helped bring the regime down in 1974.

slides.title: The Third Hellenic Republic
slides.text: The restoration of democracy in 1974 opened a new phase in modern Greek history. The Third Hellenic Republic proved far more stable than earlier constitutional experiments and anchored Greece within European institutions. Membership in the European Communities and later the eurozone tied the country more deeply to the political and economic structures of Europe.

slides.title: Crisis and Resilience in the Twenty-First Century
slides.text: The debt crisis of the 2010s brought severe austerity, unemployment, social anger, and intense international scrutiny. It was one of the most difficult periods in recent Greek history, testing both the economy and public faith in institutions. Even so, Greece remained democratic, stayed in the eurozone, and entered the 2020s still negotiating the balance between sovereignty, reform, memory, and European integration.

slides.title: The Long Greek Story
slides.text: From fragmented post-Mycenaean communities to city-states, from Macedonian conquest to Roman and Byzantine rule, from Ottoman centuries to modern nationhood, the history of Greece is unusually layered and continuous in memory even when political forms changed completely. Greece has repeatedly stood at the meeting point of Europe, the Mediterranean, and the Near East. Its history is not one single unbroken state tradition, but a long sequence of reinventions through which language, culture, religion, and historical memory remained powerful forces.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Greece's greatest achievements include the development of philosophy, drama, historical writing, political theory, classical architecture, Orthodox religious culture, and a cultural legacy that shaped Europe and the Mediterranean for more than two millennia. Its most important historical rivals included Persia, Sparta in the age of inter-Greek conflict, Rome in the struggle for independence, the Ottoman Empire during the long centuries of foreign rule, and modern geopolitical challengers in the eastern Mediterranean. Its major alliances shifted over time, from temporary coalitions among Greek states, to Byzantine Christian networks, to 19th-century support from European philhellenes, and finally to modern partnerships within the European Union, NATO, and the wider West.

slides.title: Highland Origins
slides.text: Around 1000 BCE, the peoples later identified as Israelites were part of the wider cultural world of the southern Levant. Archaeology suggests that early Israelite communities emerged in the hill country out of local Canaanite society rather than appearing suddenly from nowhere. Small agrarian settlements, shared religious traditions, and developing political identities slowly formed the basis of what later texts remembered as ancient Israel.

slides.title: From Tribal Society to Monarchy
slides.text: Biblical tradition remembers Saul, David, and Solomon as rulers of a united monarchy, though historians debate the scale and structure of that early kingdom. What is clearer is that by the early Iron Age, political centralization had increased and Jerusalem gained growing significance. Whether or not the united monarchy existed exactly as later tradition described it, this period became foundational to Jewish memory and state tradition.

slides.title: Israel and Judah
slides.text: By the 9th century BCE, two Hebrew kingdoms clearly existed: the Kingdom of Israel in the north and the Kingdom of Judah in the south. Israel was generally wealthier and more powerful, while Judah was smaller but more dynastically stable and centered on Jerusalem. Their histories were intertwined through rivalry, diplomacy, warfare, and shared religious development.

slides.title: Empires of Assyria and Babylon
slides.text: The northern Kingdom of Israel was destroyed by Assyria in the late 8th century BCE, a turning point that left a deep mark on later tradition. Judah survived longer but eventually fell to Babylon in 586 BCE, when Jerusalem and the First Temple were destroyed. These catastrophes reshaped the religious life, political imagination, and historical consciousness of the people tied to the land.

slides.title: Persian Rule and the Second Temple
slides.text: Under the Achaemenid Persian Empire, exiled elites and priestly communities were allowed to return, and the Second Temple was established in Jerusalem. The province of Yehud was small, but it became immensely important in the formation of Jewish religious texts, law, and identity. This era helped turn temple worship, sacred writing, and communal memory into durable pillars of Jewish civilization.

slides.title: Hellenistic Rule and Resistance
slides.text: After Alexander the Great, the land passed between Hellenistic powers, especially the Ptolemies and Seleucids. Greek culture left a major mark on urban life and politics, but conflict over rule and religious policy eventually produced the Maccabean revolt. That struggle created the Hasmonean state, one of the rare periods of renewed Jewish sovereignty before the modern age.

slides.title: Rome, Revolt, and Ruin
slides.text: Roman rule brought both integration into a larger imperial system and growing political tension. The First Jewish Revolt ended with the destruction of the Second Temple in 70 CE, and the Bar Kokhba revolt in the 2nd century CE brought another disastrous defeat. These events accelerated the Jewish diaspora and profoundly transformed Jewish life from temple-centered worship to rabbinic tradition.

slides.title: Late Antiquity and Byzantine Rule
slides.text: In the late Roman and Byzantine periods, the land became increasingly Christian in public life and imperial identity, though Jewish communities remained present. Jerusalem became one of Christianity's holiest cities, attracting pilgrims, churches, and imperial patronage. The region's political and sacred significance expanded even as its communities became more diverse and more tightly bound to imperial religious politics.

slides.title: Islamic Conquest and New Centers of Power
slides.text: In the 7th century, Muslim armies conquered the region, and it became part of successive Islamic caliphates. Arabic gradually became dominant, and Jerusalem gained major importance in Islamic tradition, especially with the construction of the Dome of the Rock and al-Aqsa complex. The land remained religiously mixed, but its political alignment shifted firmly into the Islamic Middle East.

slides.title: Crusaders, Ayyubids, and Mamluks
slides.text: The Crusades turned the land into a contested frontier between Latin Christian kingdoms and Muslim powers. Jerusalem changed hands, warfare damaged towns and countryside, and the region became entangled in wider struggles across the eastern Mediterranean. Later Ayyubid and Mamluk rulers reintegrated it into broader Islamic political systems while preserving its sacred importance.

slides.title: Ottoman Centuries
slides.text: From the early 16th century until World War I, the land was part of the Ottoman Empire. It was not usually the empire's political center, but cities such as Jerusalem, Safed, Hebron, and Jaffa remained important for trade, pilgrimage, and local society. Jewish, Muslim, and Christian communities all continued to live there, while the wider region evolved through imperial reform, local notables, and global economic change.

slides.title: Zionism and the British Mandate
slides.text: In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, modern Zionism emerged in Europe as a nationalist movement seeking a Jewish homeland. Under British rule after World War I, Jewish immigration increased and Arab opposition intensified, producing a deeply contested political environment. The Mandate period set the stage for the conflict between Jewish and Arab national projects that would define the modern history of the land.

slides.title: 1948 and Statehood
slides.text: In 1948, the State of Israel declared independence, and war followed immediately. For Israelis, this marked the realization of modern Jewish sovereignty after centuries of exile and persecution; for Palestinians, it coincided with dispossession and catastrophe. The war created the state, fixed early lines of conflict, and ensured that Israel's birth would remain inseparable from a broader regional struggle.

slides.title: War, Expansion, and Regional Conflict
slides.text: In the decades that followed, Israel fought repeated wars with neighboring Arab states, including the conflicts of 1956, 1967, and 1973. The 1967 war was especially decisive because Israel captured East Jerusalem, the West Bank, Gaza, the Golan Heights, and Sinai, transforming both its strategic position and the territorial dimensions of the conflict. Military success strengthened the state, but occupation and unresolved borders deepened long-term tensions.

slides.title: Society, Immigration, and State Building
slides.text: Modern Israel was shaped not only by war but also by massive immigration from Europe, the Middle East, North Africa, the former Soviet Union, and elsewhere. These migrations changed the country's language, demography, economy, and political life, while also producing social inequality and debates over identity. Israel developed strong institutions, a powerful military, a dynamic economy, and a complex internal culture that included secular, religious, Jewish, Arab, and many other communities.

slides.title: Peace Efforts and Continuing Conflict
slides.text: Israel made peace with Egypt in 1979 and with Jordan in 1994, showing that regional diplomacy was possible. Yet conflict with the Palestinians remained unresolved despite negotiations, uprisings, withdrawals, and repeated rounds of violence. Questions of sovereignty, security, settlements, refugees, and Jerusalem continue to sit at the heart of one of the world's most closely watched conflicts.

slides.title: Israel in the Present
slides.text: Today Israel is a technologically advanced, militarily powerful, and politically contentious state with a central place in regional and global affairs. Its history binds together ancient memory, religion, nationalism, migration, and modern statehood in unusually intense ways. The story of Israel from antiquity to the present is one of continuity and rupture alike, where sacred geography and modern politics remain tightly intertwined.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Israel's major historical achievements include the formation of ancient Jewish state traditions, the religious and literary legacy of the Hebrew Bible, the revival of Jewish sovereignty in the modern age, and the creation of a highly developed scientific, technological, and military state. Its principal rivals have varied from Assyria, Babylon, and Rome in antiquity to neighboring Arab states and Palestinian national movements in the modern era. Its alliances have also changed over time, but in the contemporary period they have centered especially on the United States and a widening circle of regional and international partners.

slides.title: The Nile and the Birth of Egypt
slides.text: Long before 1000 BCE, Egypt had already become one of the world's oldest and most enduring civilizations. Its development depended on the Nile, whose predictable flooding supported agriculture, population growth, and centralized rule. By the end of the 4th millennium BCE, Upper and Lower Egypt had been unified, creating the pharaonic state that would dominate Egyptian history for millennia.

slides.title: Kingship and Divine Order
slides.text: Ancient Egypt was organized around the idea that the pharaoh upheld cosmic order, justice, and stability. Monumental building, elaborate religious ritual, and a highly developed bureaucracy expressed that ideal in political form. Temples, tombs, hieroglyphic writing, and courtly administration made Egypt one of the most recognizable and symbolically rich civilizations in human history.

slides.title: A Civilization of Continuity
slides.text: What made Egypt remarkable was not only its age but its durability. Dynasties rose and fell, capitals shifted, and periods of fragmentation interrupted central rule, yet Egyptian political and religious traditions repeatedly reasserted themselves. The Nile valley gave the country a geographic coherence that helped rulers rebuild authority even after deep crises.

slides.title: Egypt Around 1000 BCE
slides.text: By 1000 BCE, Egypt was no longer in the age of pyramid-building imperial centralization, but it remained a major civilization with deep institutional memory. Power was often divided among regional rulers, priestly elites, and dynastic houses, especially after the New Kingdom. Even in periods of relative weakness, Egypt remained culturally prestigious and strategically important to the wider Near East and eastern Mediterranean.

slides.title: Libyans, Nubians, and Assyrians
slides.text: In the first millennium BCE, Egypt passed through a series of transformations shaped by both internal fragmentation and foreign intervention. Libyan-origin dynasties ruled parts of the country, Nubian kings from Kush conquered and reunified it for a time, and Assyrian invasions later disrupted Egyptian power. These centuries showed that Egypt was still vital, but no longer untouchable.

slides.title: The Late Period and Persian Rule
slides.text: Egypt experienced episodes of native revival in the Late Period, but it increasingly became the target of larger empires. The Achaemenid Persians conquered Egypt and incorporated it into an imperial system stretching across the Near East. Persian rule did not erase Egyptian identity, yet it marked another step in the transition from independent pharaonic power to rule by wider imperial states.

slides.title: Alexander and the Ptolemies
slides.text: Alexander the Great conquered Egypt in 332 BCE, and after his death the country became the core of the Ptolemaic Kingdom. Alexandria emerged as one of the greatest cities of the ancient world, famous for scholarship, trade, and royal power. Greek rulers adopted Egyptian forms of kingship even as they connected Egypt more tightly to the Hellenistic Mediterranean.

slides.title: Cleopatra and the End of Ancient Sovereignty
slides.text: The Ptolemaic kingdom eventually weakened through dynastic struggle and Roman intervention. Cleopatra VII became its most famous ruler, trying to preserve Egyptian independence in an era dominated by Roman power politics. Her defeat and death in 30 BCE marked the end of independent pharaonic-style rule and the incorporation of Egypt into the Roman Empire.

slides.title: Roman and Byzantine Egypt
slides.text: Under Roman rule, Egypt became one of the empire's most important provinces, especially as a supplier of grain. Alexandria remained a major urban and intellectual center, while Christianity spread and eventually transformed the religious landscape. In the late Roman and Byzantine periods, Egypt became a center of monasticism and Coptic Christianity, giving it a new kind of spiritual importance.

slides.title: Islamic Egypt
slides.text: In the 7th century, Arab Muslim armies conquered Egypt and brought it into the expanding Islamic world. Arabic gradually became the dominant language of public life, and new political centers such as Fustat and later Cairo reshaped the country's identity. Egypt remained a land of profound continuity, but it now belonged to a new civilizational orbit defined by Islam and the wider Middle East.

slides.title: Fatimids, Ayyubids, and Mamluks
slides.text: Medieval Egypt became one of the great power centers of the Islamic world. The Fatimids made Cairo their capital, the Ayyubids under Saladin tied Egypt to major conflicts with the Crusaders, and the Mamluks later turned it into a formidable military state. Across these centuries, Egypt controlled trade routes, sponsored scholarship, and stood at the center of politics linking Africa, the Levant, and the Red Sea.

slides.title: Ottoman Rule and Provincial Power
slides.text: The Ottomans conquered Egypt in the early 16th century, incorporating it into their empire while preserving some local elite structures. Although formally a province, Egypt remained economically and strategically significant, especially because of its agricultural wealth and position between the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean worlds. Over time, provincial rulers and military households accumulated substantial local influence.

slides.title: Muhammad Ali and Modernization
slides.text: In the 19th century, Muhammad Ali and his successors transformed Egypt through military reform, industrial and agricultural expansion, and more centralized state building. Egypt remained formally tied to the Ottoman Empire for much of this period, but in practice it became increasingly autonomous. These reforms made Egypt one of the most important states in the eastern Mediterranean, even as they also increased debt and foreign interference.

slides.title: Britain, the Canal, and Nationalism
slides.text: The construction of the Suez Canal increased Egypt's global importance but also deepened European involvement. Britain occupied Egypt in 1882 and effectively controlled it for decades, even while maintaining local dynastic institutions. Egyptian nationalism grew steadily in response, combining anti-colonial politics with calls for constitutional rule and national independence.

slides.title: Monarchy, Revolution, and Republic
slides.text: Egypt achieved formal independence in stages, but British influence and domestic instability remained strong through the monarchy. In 1952, the Free Officers overthrew King Farouk and ended the monarchy, creating a republic. This revolution remade Egyptian politics and cleared the way for a new era of Arab nationalism under Gamal Abdel Nasser.

slides.title: Nasser and Arab Leadership
slides.text: Nasser made Egypt a leading force in Arab politics and anti-colonial international movements. The nationalization of the Suez Canal in 1956 symbolized Egyptian sovereignty and defiance, while land reform, state-led development, and regional ambition reshaped society. Yet military setbacks, especially the 1967 war with Israel, also exposed the limits of the regime's vision.

slides.title: Sadat, Mubarak, and the Modern State
slides.text: Anwar Sadat redirected Egypt's course by pursuing economic opening and making peace with Israel, a move that transformed regional politics. Hosni Mubarak then presided over decades of authoritarian stability, security rule, and uneven development. Egypt remained one of the Arab world's most influential states, but political frustration and social pressure continued to build beneath the surface.

slides.title: Revolution and the Contemporary Era
slides.text: The 2011 Egyptian Revolution overthrew Mubarak and opened a short period of political uncertainty, contested elections, and competing visions of the state's future. The years that followed brought renewed military-centered rule under Abdel Fattah el-Sisi. Contemporary Egypt remains shaped by its ancient prestige, strategic geography, massive population, and enduring role as a central state of the Arab world.

slides.title: The Long Egyptian Story
slides.text: Egypt's history is one of extraordinary continuity across enormous political change. Pharaohs, Persians, Greeks, Romans, caliphs, sultans, khedives, kings, presidents, and generals all ruled the Nile valley in different ages, but Egypt repeatedly remained a major center of power, culture, and memory. Few countries link ancient civilization and modern statehood as strongly or as visibly as Egypt does.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Egypt's greatest achievements include one of the earliest and most influential state civilizations in world history, monumental architecture such as the pyramids, enduring religious and artistic traditions, the cultural leadership of Cairo, and a modern role as one of the Arab world's central states. Its rivals across history ranged from Assyrians, Persians, and Romans to Crusader powers, imperial competitors, and modern regional challengers. Its alliances shifted with every era, but Egypt has repeatedly acted as a bridge linking Africa, the Mediterranean, the Islamic world, and the modern international system.

name: United Kingdom
aliases: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland

slides.title: From Kingdoms to a Realm
slides.text: The history of the United Kingdom begins long before the modern state itself, in the separate histories of England, Scotland, Wales, and Ireland. Roman rule shaped parts of Britain, Anglo-Saxon kingdoms emerged after Rome's withdrawal, and later Viking and Norman conquests transformed politics, language, and landholding. By the late medieval period, England and Scotland were established kingdoms with intertwined but distinct political paths.

slides.title: Tudor, Stuart, and Union
slides.text: The early modern period brought religious upheaval, stronger monarchy, and eventually civil war and constitutional change. The crowns of England and Scotland were united in 1603 under James VI and I, and the political union of 1707 created the Kingdom of Great Britain. This was a decisive step toward the modern British state and the institutions that would shape its global role.

slides.title: Empire and Industrial Power
slides.text: From the 18th through the 19th centuries, Britain became the world's leading naval, commercial, and imperial power. The Industrial Revolution transformed its economy and society, while imperial expansion linked Britain to every continent. The United Kingdom emerged as a center of finance, industry, parliamentary development, and global influence, though that power depended heavily on empire and colonial domination.

slides.title: War, Welfare, and Decline of Empire
slides.text: The two world wars reshaped Britain profoundly. Victory came at enormous cost, and after 1945 the empire rapidly dissolved as colonies became independent. At the same time, the postwar state created the welfare system and the National Health Service, redefining the social contract at home even as Britain's global role narrowed.

slides.title: Europe, Identity, and Modern Politics
slides.text: In the late 20th and early 21st centuries, the United Kingdom balanced close ties with Europe, a strong relationship with the United States, and its own domestic constitutional tensions. Devolution gave Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland their own institutions, while Brexit reopened major questions about sovereignty, trade, and the future shape of the union itself. Modern Britain remains influential, but more contested in identity and direction than at the height of empire.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Britain's major achievements include parliamentary constitutional development, industrialization, naval power, scientific influence, global finance, and outsized cultural reach. Its major rivals across history included France, Spain, Germany, and various anti-colonial movements across the empire. Its most important alliances evolved from European balance-of-power coalitions to the modern Atlantic partnership with NATO and especially the United States.

slides.title: Early Ireland
slides.text: Ireland's early history developed outside the Roman Empire, with political life organized around local kingdoms, dynasties, and monastic centers rather than a single centralized state. Christianity transformed the island in late antiquity and the early medieval period, making Ireland an important center of learning and religious culture. Gaelic political and social traditions remained strong for centuries despite Viking settlement and outside pressure.

slides.title: English Intervention and Conquest
slides.text: From the late 12th century onward, English intervention slowly expanded into domination, though full control remained incomplete for long periods. Tudor and Stuart policy pushed harder toward conquest, plantation, and religious restructuring, especially in Ireland's northern regions. These centuries laid the foundations for many of the island's later political and sectarian divisions.

slides.title: Union, Famine, and Nationalism
slides.text: The Act of Union in 1801 folded Ireland directly into the United Kingdom, but political union did not resolve inequality or land conflict. The Great Famine of the 1840s was a defining disaster, causing mass death, migration, and lasting trauma. In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Irish nationalism intensified through constitutional politics, cultural revival, and revolutionary activism.

slides.title: Independence and Partition
slides.text: The Easter Rising of 1916, the War of Independence, and the Anglo-Irish Treaty transformed Ireland's future. Most of the island became the Irish Free State, later the Republic of Ireland, while partition left Northern Ireland within the United Kingdom. Independence brought sovereignty, but partition ensured that the Irish question remained unfinished.

slides.title: Republic, Europe, and Modern Ireland
slides.text: In the later 20th century, the Republic of Ireland evolved from a relatively poor and conservative state into a more open, globally connected society. Membership in the European Communities and later the European Union reshaped its economy and strategic outlook, while the Good Friday Agreement helped transform relations on the island. Modern Ireland is defined by sovereignty, European integration, and a strong awareness of historical memory.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Ireland's major achievements include preserving a distinct national identity under centuries of pressure, securing independence, helping stabilize Northern Ireland through diplomacy, and building a successful modern European economy. Its main historical rival was British domination in its various forms, especially in the eras of conquest, union, and partition. In the modern period, Ireland's strongest alliances have been with the European Union and close democratic partners, while retaining military neutrality outside NATO.

slides.title: From Gaul to Kingdom
slides.text: France's roots lie in Roman Gaul, the Frankish kingdoms, and the slow consolidation of royal power in the medieval west. The Capetian monarchy gradually extended control over territories that had once been highly fragmented. By the late Middle Ages, France was emerging as one of Europe's strongest and most centralized kingdoms.

slides.title: Monarchy and State Building
slides.text: The Valois and Bourbon eras deepened state centralization, court culture, and military power. France became a dominant force in European diplomacy and warfare, often competing with the Habsburgs and Britain. Absolutism under Louis XIV symbolized both the grandeur and the strains of the old regime.

slides.title: Revolution and Empire
slides.text: The French Revolution transformed not only France but the political language of the modern world. Monarchy gave way to republic, terror, war, and eventually Napoleonic empire, while concepts such as citizenship, secular politics, and national mobilization spread across Europe. The revolution permanently changed the relationship between state and society in France.

slides.title: Republics, Empire, and World Wars
slides.text: The 19th century and early 20th century brought alternating regimes, colonial expansion, and repeated conflict with Germany. France built one of the world's largest overseas empires but also endured devastating losses in World War I and defeat and occupation in World War II. Liberation and reconstruction then pushed France toward a new republican settlement.

slides.title: The Fifth Republic and Europe
slides.text: Since 1958, the Fifth Republic has combined a strong presidency with parliamentary institutions and has made France a central actor in European integration. Decolonization, especially the Algerian war, marked a turning point in its global role. Modern France remains a nuclear power, a founding EU state, and one of the key political centers of Europe.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: France's major achievements include legal and political influence through the Revolution, major contributions to art, philosophy, science, diplomacy, and the building of modern Europe. Its main rivals across history included England or Britain, Habsburg powers, and later Germany. Its main alliances eventually shifted toward NATO, the European Union, and a deep partnership with Germany as a foundation for postwar Europe.

slides.title: From Iberia to Christian Kingdoms
slides.text: Spain's history grew out of Roman Hispania, Visigothic rule, and the Islamic conquest of much of the peninsula in the 8th century. For centuries, Muslim and Christian polities coexisted, competed, and influenced one another across Iberia. The eventual rise of Castile, Aragon, and other Christian kingdoms laid the groundwork for political unification.

slides.title: Unification and Global Empire
slides.text: The union of Ferdinand and Isabella and the completion of the Reconquista in 1492 marked a major turning point. In the same era, Spain launched an overseas empire that became one of the largest in world history, especially in the Americas. Spain became a dominant European and global power, though imperial greatness came with military overreach and fiscal strain.

slides.title: Empire, Decline, and Reform
slides.text: In the 16th and 17th centuries, Spain remained a major power but struggled with repeated wars, economic difficulties, and competition from rival states. By the 18th and 19th centuries, reformist efforts collided with imperial decline and political instability. The loss of much of the overseas empire transformed Spain's place in the world.

slides.title: Civil War and Dictatorship
slides.text: The 20th century brought one of Spain's defining traumas: the Civil War of 1936-1939, followed by the Franco dictatorship. The conflict left lasting ideological and social scars, and the regime repressed political opposition while keeping Spain apart from much of democratic postwar Europe. This period remains central to modern Spanish memory.

slides.title: Democracy and European Integration
slides.text: After Franco's death, Spain transitioned to democracy and built a constitutional monarchy with strong regional autonomy structures. Entry into the European Communities accelerated modernization and tied Spain more deeply to the European project. Contemporary Spain balances democracy, regional tensions, tourism, trade, and a strong cultural presence in the Spanish-speaking world.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Spain's major achievements include creating one of the early modern world's largest empires, spreading the Spanish language globally, and building a modern democratic state after dictatorship. Its main rivals at different times included Islamic states in Iberia, France, England or Britain, and other European imperial competitors. Its modern alliances rest mainly on NATO, the European Union, and broad transatlantic cooperation.

slides.title: Formation of Portugal
slides.text: Portugal emerged in the western Iberian frontier during the medieval struggle between Christian and Muslim powers. It secured independence from Leon and Castile and gradually established itself as a distinct Atlantic kingdom. Its relatively stable borders became one of its most enduring historical features.

slides.title: Atlantic Expansion
slides.text: In the 15th and 16th centuries, Portugal became a pioneer of oceanic exploration. Portuguese sailors opened maritime routes around Africa to Asia, established trading posts from Brazil to India, and built one of the earliest global empires. Navigation, cartography, and seaborne commerce made Portugal historically far larger in influence than in size.

slides.title: Empire and Constraint
slides.text: Portugal's empire brought wealth and prestige but also dependence on trade networks and vulnerability to stronger European rivals. The Iberian Union, wars, and colonial competition challenged Portuguese autonomy. Over time, maintaining overseas holdings became increasingly difficult, especially as larger imperial powers rose.

slides.title: Monarchy, Republic, and Dictatorship
slides.text: The 19th and early 20th centuries brought constitutional struggle, imperial strain, and the fall of the monarchy. Portugal became a republic but soon experienced instability, eventually followed by the Estado Novo dictatorship under Salazar. The regime preserved empire for a time but at the cost of repression and political stagnation.

slides.title: Democracy and Europe
slides.text: The Carnation Revolution of 1974 ended dictatorship and accelerated decolonization across the Portuguese empire. Portugal then consolidated democracy and joined the European Communities, reshaping its economy and political orientation. Modern Portugal is both Atlantic and European, with influence extending through language, diaspora, and historical memory.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Portugal's greatest achievements include pioneering early global sea routes, building a vast maritime empire, and achieving a peaceful democratic transition in the 20th century. Its major rivals historically included Castile or Spain, the Dutch, and other imperial competitors at sea. Its enduring alliances include the historic Anglo-Portuguese alliance and modern membership in NATO and the European Union.

slides.title: Crossroads of Europe
slides.text: The territory of modern Belgium has long been a borderland shaped by Roman, Frankish, Burgundian, Spanish, Austrian, French, and Dutch rule. Its location between major European powers gave it strategic and economic importance but also exposed it repeatedly to invasion and contestation. Medieval cities in the Low Countries became among Europe's most commercial and urbanized centers.

slides.title: Revolution and Independence
slides.text: Modern Belgium emerged in 1830 after revolution against the United Kingdom of the Netherlands. The new kingdom developed as a constitutional monarchy and industrial power, especially in coal, steel, and manufacturing. Belgium's creation reflected both local identity and great-power diplomacy in a fragile European balance.

slides.title: Industry, Empire, and Congo
slides.text: Belgium industrialized rapidly and became one of continental Europe's early industrial economies. At the same time, Belgian imperial rule in the Congo became one of the darkest chapters in modern colonial history, especially under Leopold II. Colonial wealth and brutality both shaped the country's global legacy.

slides.title: World Wars and Occupation
slides.text: Belgium's strategic position made it a battleground in both world wars, and German invasions in 1914 and 1940 left deep marks on national memory. Despite formal neutrality before World War I, geography repeatedly exposed Belgium to the ambitions of larger neighbors. Postwar reconstruction pushed the country strongly toward international cooperation.

slides.title: Federal Belgium and Europe
slides.text: Modern Belgium evolved into a federal state structured around linguistic and regional compromise between Dutch-speaking Flanders, French-speaking Wallonia, and bilingual Brussels. It also became one of the symbolic capitals of European integration, hosting major EU and NATO institutions. Belgium today is both internally complex and internationally central to European diplomacy.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Belgium's major achievements include early industrial development, constitutional monarchy, and its role as a core diplomatic center of modern Europe. Its main historical dangers came less from one permanent enemy than from being caught between stronger powers such as France and Germany. Its modern alliances are deeply tied to the EU, NATO, and Benelux cooperation.

slides.title: Low Countries and Revolt
slides.text: The Netherlands emerged from the political and commercial world of the Low Countries, one of medieval Europe's richest regions. In the 16th century, revolt against Habsburg Spain created the Dutch Republic, which combined military resistance with a new political culture. Independence transformed the northern provinces into one of the most dynamic states of early modern Europe.

slides.title: The Dutch Golden Age
slides.text: In the 17th century, the Dutch Republic became a center of global trade, finance, shipbuilding, and art. Amsterdam stood at the heart of world commerce, while Dutch merchants and chartered companies built a wide colonial and maritime presence. The era produced extraordinary cultural figures and a powerful republican-commercial identity.

slides.title: Empire, Rivalry, and Change
slides.text: Dutch power eventually faced stiff competition from England or Britain and France, while internal political struggles shaped the republic's institutions. Though the Netherlands lost some early dominance, it retained major commercial and colonial importance, especially in Asia and the Caribbean. The Napoleonic era brought major restructuring and helped prepare the way for monarchy.

slides.title: Kingdom and Modernization
slides.text: The modern Kingdom of the Netherlands emerged in the 19th century, though Belgium's secession quickly reshaped it. Dutch society industrialized, built a strong trading economy, and maintained overseas possessions, particularly the Dutch East Indies. Its modern identity mixed monarchy, commerce, and careful diplomacy.

slides.title: War, Reconstruction, and Europe
slides.text: The Netherlands was occupied by Nazi Germany in World War II, an experience that deeply affected its political culture and historical memory. After the war, decolonization, especially Indonesian independence, reshaped the kingdom's global role. In the postwar era, the Netherlands became a strong supporter of European integration, Atlantic alliance, and international law.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Dutch achievements include global trade networks, financial innovation, maritime influence, major artistic contributions, and an enduring role in international law and diplomacy. Its main historical rivals included Spain, England or Britain, and France in different eras of European conflict. Today its strongest alliances lie in the EU, NATO, and Benelux cooperation.

slides.title: Small State in a Strategic Zone
slides.text: Luxembourg's history is shaped by its position between larger powers in western Europe. Emerging from a medieval county and duchy, it became important less through size than through strategic geography and dynastic linkages. Repeatedly contested, it survived by adaptation, diplomacy, and institutional continuity.

slides.title: Dynasty, Fortress, and Contest
slides.text: In the medieval and early modern periods, Luxembourg was tied to wider imperial and dynastic structures. Its fortress made it one of Europe's strategic strongpoints, and neighboring powers repeatedly sought influence over it. This history left Luxembourg deeply aware of the dangers of great-power rivalry.

slides.title: Independence in the Nineteenth Century
slides.text: Luxembourg's modern political form emerged through 19th-century diplomatic settlements that recognized its distinct status while limiting its vulnerability. Though small, it built durable institutions and a constitutional monarchy under the grand duchy. Geography still mattered, but survival increasingly depended on international guarantees and legal recognition.

slides.title: War and Occupation
slides.text: Luxembourg was occupied in both world wars, a reminder that neutrality alone could not protect a strategically placed small state. These experiences pushed it firmly toward postwar cooperation with neighboring democracies. Security and integration became central pillars of its modern strategy.

slides.title: Europe and Finance
slides.text: In the postwar era, Luxembourg became one of the founding centers of European integration and later a major financial hub. Its influence came from institutions, diplomacy, and economic specialization rather than military power. Modern Luxembourg is a small state with outsized importance in European governance.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Luxembourg's achievements include maintaining state continuity despite its size, helping found European integration, and becoming a major financial and diplomatic center. Its principal historical threats came from larger surrounding powers rather than one fixed enemy. Its modern alliances are deeply rooted in the EU, NATO, and Benelux framework.

slides.title: From the Holy Roman Empire to the German Lands
slides.text: Germany's history cannot be reduced to a single ancient state, because for centuries the German lands existed within the loose framework of the Holy Roman Empire. Political authority was fragmented among princes, bishops, cities, and dynasties, yet language, law, and economic ties still linked the region. This long history of decentralization shaped later debates over what Germany should become.

slides.title: Reformation and State Power
slides.text: The Reformation transformed the German lands religiously and politically, creating deep confessional divisions and contributing to major wars such as the Thirty Years' War. Over time, states such as Prussia and Austria emerged as major powers within the German-speaking world. The struggle between fragmentation and centralization remained a defining German theme.

slides.title: Unification and Empire
slides.text: In the 19th century, industrialization, nationalism, and diplomacy under Bismarck led to German unification under Prussian leadership in 1871. The new German Empire quickly became one of Europe's strongest industrial and military powers. Its rise transformed the balance of power on the continent and intensified rivalry with neighboring states.

slides.title: War, Nazism, and Catastrophe
slides.text: Germany's history in the first half of the 20th century is inseparable from World War I, the Weimar crisis, the rise of Nazism, the Holocaust, and World War II. These events brought devastation to Europe and left Germany morally, politically, and physically shattered. No account of modern Germany can avoid the scale of that catastrophe.

slides.title: Division, Reunification, and Europe
slides.text: After 1945, Germany was divided into East and West, each embedded in opposing Cold War blocs. West Germany became a democratic and economically powerful state deeply tied to European integration, while East Germany remained under communist rule until 1989. Reunification in 1990 restored political unity and made Germany the central economic power of the European Union.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Germany's achievements include major contributions to philosophy, science, music, industrial development, and postwar democratic reconstruction. Its most important historical rivals included France, Austria in some phases of German politics, Britain in the era of naval competition, and the Soviet Union in the age of total war. Its modern alliances are anchored in the EU, NATO, and especially postwar reconciliation with France and partnership with the United States.

slides.title: Dynastic Heartland
slides.text: Austria's history is closely tied to the Habsburg dynasty, which turned a regional power base into one of Europe's most important imperial houses. For centuries Austria was central to the Holy Roman Empire and later to the Habsburg Monarchy. Vienna emerged as a political and cultural capital of continental importance.

slides.title: Empire and Multiethnic Rule
slides.text: Habsburg Austria ruled over a highly diverse collection of peoples and territories in central and eastern Europe. Managing that diversity required diplomacy, military strength, and administrative adaptation, but it also created recurring tensions. Austria's role was often that of a conservative great power trying to preserve order in a rapidly changing continent.

slides.title: Austria-Hungary and Collapse
slides.text: The 1867 compromise created Austria-Hungary, a dual monarchy that remained one of Europe's major powers until World War I. The empire's collapse in 1918 ended Austria's role as the center of a multinational imperial system and left a much smaller republic. This was one of the sharpest transformations in modern European history.

slides.title: Anschluss and War
slides.text: Interwar Austria struggled with instability and authoritarian politics before Nazi Germany absorbed it in the Anschluss of 1938. The experience of annexation, dictatorship, and war deeply shaped the country's later political identity. After 1945, Austria rebuilt itself under Allied occupation and carefully redefined its place in Europe.

slides.title: Neutrality and Modern Europe
slides.text: Postwar Austria adopted neutrality as a core element of its identity while also integrating economically and politically into western Europe. It developed into a prosperous democratic state with strong cultural influence and an important role in diplomacy. Membership in the European Union later gave Austria a new framework for regional cooperation while preserving its non-NATO stance.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Austria's achievements include centuries of dynastic statecraft, major influence in music and culture, and the successful construction of a stable modern republic after imperial collapse. Its historical rivals shifted across time, including the Ottoman Empire, Prussia, France under Napoleon, and Germany in the era of Anschluss. Its modern alliances are centered on the EU and close European cooperation, while maintaining military neutrality outside NATO.

slides.title: Confederation in the Alps
slides.text: Switzerland emerged from alpine communities and cantonal alliances that sought autonomy within the wider Holy Roman imperial world. Over time, the confederation developed through military cooperation, negotiated independence, and local self-rule. Geography, federalism, and civic traditions became central to Swiss political identity.

slides.title: Cantons, Militias, and Balance
slides.text: The old Swiss Confederacy grew through alliances among cantons rather than through centralized monarchy. Military reputation and mercenary service gave the Swiss a distinct place in European politics, but internal religious divisions and regional differences required constant balancing. This decentralized legacy still shapes Swiss politics today.

slides.title: Neutrality and Federal Statehood
slides.text: Modern Switzerland took shape in the 19th century as a federal state after internal conflict and constitutional transformation. Neutrality became one of its defining international principles, helping it avoid entanglement in many European wars. Federal institutions and direct democracy gave the country a distinctive political model.

slides.title: Survival in a Violent Europe
slides.text: Switzerland remained outside both world wars, though not outside their pressures. Its strategic position, military preparedness, and diplomatic choices helped preserve independence, while its financial and humanitarian roles gave it unusual international visibility. Neutrality became both a practical policy and a national myth.

slides.title: Modern Switzerland
slides.text: Today Switzerland is known for federalism, banking and finance, high-value industry, diplomacy, and international institutions. Though not a member of the EU or NATO, it is deeply connected to European markets and governance through other arrangements. Modern Swiss identity continues to rest on neutrality, multilingualism, and political stability.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Switzerland's achievements include durable federal democracy, neutrality, humanitarian diplomacy, and global financial and technical influence. It had important historical conflicts with Habsburg and regional powers but has not been defined by one lasting modern enemy. Its alliances today are best understood as economic and diplomatic ties with Europe and the wider West rather than military bloc membership.

slides.title: Roman Legacy and Fragmented Italy
slides.text: Italy's history begins with the legacy of Rome, whose empire shaped law, language, urban life, and political imagination across Europe and the Mediterranean. After the fall of the western empire, the Italian peninsula fragmented into kingdoms, communes, papal territories, and foreign-dominated states. For centuries, Italy was culturally central but politically divided.

slides.title: Cities, Commerce, and the Renaissance
slides.text: Medieval and Renaissance Italy produced some of Europe's richest cities and most influential artistic and intellectual movements. Venice, Florence, Milan, Genoa, Rome, and others became centers of trade, banking, diplomacy, and artistic patronage. The Renaissance made the peninsula one of the defining cultural engines of world history.

slides.title: Foreign Rule and the Long Road to Unity
slides.text: Despite its cultural prestige, Italy remained politically fragmented and often subject to outside influence from France, Spain, Austria, and others. The 19th-century Risorgimento sought to unite the peninsula into one nation-state. Unification under the Kingdom of Italy transformed political geography but left major regional and social divisions unresolved.

slides.title: War, Fascism, and Rupture
slides.text: In the 20th century, Italy experienced World War I, fascism under Mussolini, imperial ambition, and disastrous participation in World War II. The fall of fascism and the collapse of monarchy led to the creation of the republic in 1946. This rupture remains central to modern Italian political identity.

slides.title: Republic and European Role
slides.text: Postwar Italy rebuilt itself as a democratic republic and became a founding state of European integration. It developed into a major industrial and cultural power while also grappling with political fragmentation, corruption, regional inequality, and economic strain. Modern Italy is at once heir to Rome, the Renaissance, Catholic tradition, and the postwar European order.

slides.title: Achievements, Rivals, and Alliances
slides.text: Italy's great achievements include Roman civilization, the Renaissance, immense artistic and architectural influence, and its role as a founding state of the modern European project. Its major rivals shifted across history, including Carthage, foreign dynasties competing for the peninsula, Austria, and various European great powers. Its modern alliances are firmly rooted in the EU, NATO, and close western cooperation.
